snippet.title: Introduction to Ham Radio: Your First Steps into the Airwaves (Module 1)
snippet.description:
Welcome to Module 1 of our comprehensive Ham Radio course! In this video, we'll take a foundational look at the fascinating world of Amateur Radio, often called "Ham Radio." Whether you're a complete beginner or just curious, this module is designed to give you a solid understanding of the basics and light a fire in your curiosity.

What We Cover in This Module:

What is Ham Radio? We'll define amateur radio, explore its history, and discuss why millions of people around the world are captivated by this hobby. (It's not just about talking to people, though that's pretty cool!)

Why is it called "Ham Radio"? We'll debunk a few myths and explore the most likely origins of this unique nickname. (Spoiler: It probably doesn't have much to do with actual pork.)

What Can You Do with Ham Radio? We'll showcase the incredibly diverse range of activities you can explore, including:

Local Communications: Talk to other Hams in your area using handheld, mobile, or base station radios.

Global DXing: Connect with people in distant countries on the other side of the planet using shortwave radio waves that bounce off the ionosphere.

Emergency Communications: Learn how Hams play a vital role in providing critical communication during disasters when other systems fail. (It's like being a communication superhero!)

Digital Modes: Explore the cutting-edge of radio technology using computers to send text, images, and data around the world. (No morse code proficiency required!)

Satellite Communications: Talk to satellites and even the International Space Station! (Okay, this one is pretty nerdy, but also incredibly awesome.)

Contesting and Radiosport: Compete in friendly competitions to see who can make the most contacts or connect with specific locations.

And So Much More! From experimenting with antennas to building your own gear, the possibilities are truly endless.

The Ham Radio Community: Discover the welcoming and supportive global community of Hams who are always eager to help newcomers. (It's a "very" friendly group, despite the "ham" name.)

How to Get Started: We'll provide a high-level overview of the license requirements in the United States and point you toward resources for further learning and study. (Getting your license is easier than you might think!)

This module is your springboard into the exciting world of amateur radio. So, grab a cup of coffee (or tea!), settle in, and get ready to be inspired!

What to Expect in Module 2: In the next video, we'll dive deeper into the different types of radios, antennas, and equipment you'll need to get on the air. We'll also start exploring the fundamental concepts of radio waves and electronics in a beginner-friendly way.

About This Series: This video is part of a multi-part series designed to guide you through the essentials of Ham Radio and help you prepare for your technician class license. Stay tuned for more informative and engaging videos!

Hashtags: #HamRadio #AmateurRadio #HamRadioBeginner #IntroductionToHamRadio #HamRadioCourse #GettingStartedInHamRadio #RadioLab #Free #Radiosport #DXing #EmergencyComm #DigitalModes #SatelliteComms #GetLicensed

Remember to like and subscribe for more Ham Radio content!

snippet.title: Moja pierwsza aktywacja SOTA SP/BZ-074 | Beskid Wyspowy | Radioamatorska wyprawa w góry
snippet.description:
📡 Moja pierwsza aktywacja SOTA SP/BZ-074 w Beskidzie Wyspowym!

W tym filmie pokazuję moją pierwszą w życiu aktywację SOTA (Summits On The Air) ze szczytu SP/BZ-074 w pięknym paśmie Beskidu Wyspowego. To była wyjątkowa radioamatorska przygoda – połączenie górskiej wyprawy, krótkofalarstwa i pracy w terenie.

Podczas aktywacji korzystałem z przenośnej stacji radiowej YAESU FTX1 i pracowałem na pasmach VHF/UHF, nawiązując łączności z innymi krótkofalowcami. Pokazuję również sprzęt, przygotowania oraz sam przebieg pracy na szczycie.

snippet.title: This Happened When I Used the IC-7300 MK2 in a Real Park
snippet.description:
Take your amateur radio adventures to the next level with this in-depth Parks on the Air (POTA) activation featuring the Icom IC-7300 MK2! In this video, I head out into the field to put the IC-7300MK2 HF/50MHz transceiver to the test in a real-world portable operating environment.
This activation focuses on evaluating the voice (SSB) performance, audio clarity, and receive sensitivity of the Icom 7300 MK2 while making live contacts from a POTA-designated park. You’ll hear real QSOs, band conditions, signal reports, and how the radio performs under actual operating conditions — not just in the shack.
Whether you’re a ham radio operator, shortwave listener, or considering upgrading to the IC-7300MK2, this video gives you valuable insight into:• Transmit audio quality and microphone performance• Receive filtering, noise reduction, and weak signal handling• Real-world HF band conditions during a POTA activation• Portable ham radio setup and field operating tips• Parks on the Air activation workflow and logging
The Icom IC-7300 series has been a favorite among amateur radio operators for its SDR architecture, spectrum scope, and ease of use — but how does the MK2 version perform in the field? Let’s find out.
If you're into ham radio, POTA activations, portable HF operations, DXing, or radio gear reviews, this video is for you!

snippet.title: Ham Radios Could Cause Airplanes To Crash! Short-Format Amateur Radio Callsigns & The FCC Gets Sued
snippet.description:
00:00 The FCC Gets Sued
01:01 Shorter Is Better!
01:23 Spurious CW Emissions
02:02 Sign-Off
02:11 Mysterious CW Message
02:16 Classic Baofeng UV-5R Roger Beep Melody

In This weeks Radio Dork news A U.S. District Court judge has denied a motion by the FCC to dismiss a case challenging the FCC's denial of an amateur radio license application to a reddit user that goes by the name "CaptKirk2021".  The ruling, issued by Washington DC District Court Judge Ana C. Reyes, marks a key early victory in the case challenging FCC character qualification reviews for amateur radio licenses.
The suit, initially filed in October of 2024, alleges that the FCC is attempting to deny the plaintiff's Amateur Radio license application without providing an evidentiary hearing, which the plaintiff, CaptKirk2021, claims is required under the Administrative Procedure Act of 1946, as well as the FCC's own stated Character Policy. You can follow this ongoing case.. on Reddit..

Over 250 previously held short-format amateur radio callsigns have just become available for vanity applications, after the expiration of the two-year grace period and many processing delays. Extra-class amateur radio licensees can now apply for these highly sought-after callsigns via the FCC ULS system. 

Ham radio CW signals, also known as Morse Code, have been found to interfere with certain transponders on Boeing 787 Dreamliners, contributing to an $8 million dollar repair bill for several airlines, including American, United, and Alaska Air. As reported by the PaddleYourOwnKanoo..com website, the issue was discovered after the FAA received reports of Mode-S transponders no longer replying to radar interrogations, resulting in the jetliners becoming invisible to air-traffic controllers, which could lead to a mid-air collision, or worse.

snippet.title: The Ultimate POTA Hunting Cheat Code - How to Use POTAPlus!
snippet.description:
Are you tired of frantically spinning your VFO dial and manually typing in park numbers before the activator disappears into the QSB? Whether you're chasing a rare activation in a remote Provincial Park or working a massive pileup for a Parks Canada site, POTAPlus is an absolute game-changer for Canadian amateur radio operators.

In this comprehensive video, I'll show you exactly how to install and set up this amazing free browser extension that completely transforms the way you hunt Parks on the Air (POTA). POTAPlus seamlessly integrates with the official POTA spotting page to give you a massive operating advantage. It brilliantly colour-codes your needed parks (perfect for tracking down those ATNOs or grabbing that last needed province), tunes your radio instantly with a single click of the "QSY" button, and automatically pushes your contact details straight to your local logging software and directly up to your QRZ logbook with the "QSL" button.

We will dive deep into the entire setup process. I'll walk you step-by-step through installing the "unpacked" extension in your browser, downloading and running the essential background proxy app (which bridges the gap between your web browser and your transceiver), and configuring your CAT rig control. Finally, we'll cover setting up that seamless QRZ integration, so your logs are instantly synced and secured. Stick around until the end for a look at my favourite hidden bonus feature: The POTA Scanner, which automatically sweeps through active spots while you sit back with a double-double and listen for those VE, VA, and VY callsigns to roll in!

👇 Links & Resources Mentioned in this Video: 👇

Download POTAPlus: https://dwestbrook.net/projects/potaplus/
QRZ: https://www.qrz.com/
Official POTA Website: https://parksontheair.com/

👍 If you found this helpful, please LIKE the video and SUBSCRIBE for more Ham Radio tips, tricks, and tutorials from north of the border!

snippet.title: ZEUS DX-500 600W HF Linear Amplifier: Unboxing, Teardown & 100W vs 600W Test
snippet.description:
ZEUS DX-500 600W HF linear amplifier unboxing, teardown and real power test! See 100W vs 600W on online SDR receiver + inside look at build quality, automatic band switching, LAN/web control and more.
In this video I unbox my brand new ZEUS DX-500 (also called ZEUS DX500) from DX World (Greece). This is a 500–600W (up to ~700W on some bands) HF linear amplifier with many modern features:
• Automatic band switching – no CAT cable needed
• Dual antenna ports (SO-239) with auto/manual selection
• Built-in LAN connector for web interface control & online firmware updates
• Temperature-controlled big 11 cm fan
• Protections: SWR, overcurrent, overheat, wrong antenna, LPF error
• Requires external 50–53V power supply (tested with Huawei R4850G2 / MeanWell clone ~3kW)

I void the warranty immediately and show you the full interior, components and build quality.
Later I compare output on a real online SDR receiver: 100W barefoot vs ~600W with the amp on HF bands.

If you're into ham radio, DXing or looking for affordable HF linear amplifier in 2026 – this might be interesting alternative.

3:45 Rear panel & features
7:20 Power supply discussion
12:10 Opening the case – teardown
18:50 Inside look & build quality
24:30 Fan noise test

32:15 100W vs 600W comparison on SDR

Idaho Electrician 2026 Licensing Guide | Requirements, Exams & NEC Updates
Thinking about becoming a licensed electrician in Idaho in 2026? This complete guide walks you through Idaho’s electrical licensing process — including license types, experience requirements, exam structure, and the latest NEC updates that directly affect your test and field work. ⚡

Whether you’re starting as an apprentice or moving up to Journeyman or Master Electrician, this video gives you a clear roadmap from application to passing your exam.

In this video you’ll learn:

✅ Idaho electrician license classifications (Apprentice, Journeyman, Master, Electrical Contractor)
✅ Required work experience and eligibility for each license level
✅ How to apply for licensure through the Idaho Division of Building Safety
✅ What’s on the Idaho electrical licensing exams
✅ Passing score requirements and exam format
✅ Who administers the exams (PSI testing centers)
✅ Reference materials allowed during the open-book exams
✅ Important NEC code updates impacting Idaho in 2026
✅ License renewal and continuing education basics
✅ Exam prep tips to help you pass on your first attempt

Idaho Electrical Licensing Overview

In Idaho, electricians are licensed at the state level through the Idaho Division of Building Safety (DBS). All Journeyman, Master, and Electrical Contractors must be licensed before performing electrical work independently or pulling permits.

Idaho Electrical Exam Breakdown

• Exams are administered by PSI Exams
• Minimum passing score is typically 70%
• Exams are open book and reference the National Electrical Code (NEC)
• Topics include:

🔹 Electrical theory
🔹 Wiring methods & conductors
🔹 Grounding and bonding
🔹 Overcurrent protection
🔹 Services & feeders
🔹 Motors and controls
🔹 Safety & OSHA fundamentals
🔹 Idaho electrical laws and rules

📘 NEC Updates for 2026

Idaho electrical exams and field compliance continue to align with the most current NEC standards adopted by the state. These updates affect:

⚡ GFCI & AFCI requirements
⚡ Surge protection
⚡ Grounding and bonding rules
⚡ Energy storage systems
⚡ Residential & commercial safety changes

Understanding these NEC changes is critical — both for passing your exam and staying compliant on real job sites.

snippet.title: Why POTA Hams Love Wolf River Coil Antennas | Hamcation 2026
snippet.description:
Why do so many Parks on the Air (POTA) operators choose Wolf River Coil antennas for portable HF operations?

At Orlando Hamcation 2026, Professor Jim (N4BFR) from Ham Radio Prep sits down with Terry (N9AOT) from Wolf River Coils to talk about why these antennas are so popular with portable and mobile operators.

Wolf River Coil antennas are adjustable, portable antenna systems that allow operators to tune across multiple HF bands by sliding a collar along the coil — making them simple and flexible for field operations.  ￼

In this interview, we discuss:

📡 Why POTA operators prefer coil antennas
⚡ How these antennas tune multiple bands
🌲 Portable setups for Parks on the Air
🚗 Mobile vs portable antenna configurations
🔧 Different antenna kits and accessories

If you’re interested in portable HF operation, field setups, or POTA activations, this interview gives a great look at one of the most popular antenna options used by activators.

At Orlando Hamcation 2026, Professor Jim (N4BFR) from Ham Radio Prep sits down with Terry (N9AOT) from Wolf River Coils to talk about why these antennas are so popular with portable and mobile operators.

Wolf River Coil antennas are adjustable, portable antenna systems that allow operators to tune across multiple HF bands by sliding a collar along the coil — making them simple and flexible for field operations.  ￼

In this interview, we discuss:

📡 Why POTA operators prefer coil antennas
⚡ How these antennas tune multiple bands
🌲 Portable setups for Parks on the Air
🚗 Mobile vs portable antenna configurations
🔧 Different antenna kits and accessories

If you’re interested in portable HF operation, field setups, or POTA activations, this interview gives a great look at one of the most popular antenna options used by activators.

snippet.title: What Actually Counts as a POTA Contact?
snippet.description:
New to Parks On The Air (POTA) or just need a quick refresher on the rules? In this video, we break down exactly what makes a contact valid according to the POTA guidelines. Whether you are an Activator out in the field or a Hunter sitting in your shack, understanding these simple rules will ensure every QSO counts toward your awards!

🔍 In This Video:

We clear up the confusion around what modes are allowed, the "no-repeater" rule, and how you can find contacts without even calling "CQ POTA." We'll cover everything from satellite operations to working contest stations while you're at a park.

✅ The Quick Checklist for a Valid Contact:

Location: The Activator must be physically located within the boundary of an approved POTA reference (park).

Mode: Any legal amateur radio mode is allowed (SSB, CW, FT8, FM, etc.).

Simplex Only: No terrestrial repeaters! While repeaters are great for local chat, they aren't allowed for POTA credits.

Satellites are OK: Despite the no-repeater rule, Bird-based contacts (Satellites) are perfectly valid.

Who can you work?: Anyone! You can work contest stations, DX, or anyone calling CQ. They don’t have to be participating in POTA for the contact to count for you.

Calling CQ: You don’t have to call "CQ POTA," though most activators do to help hunters find them.

🔗 Helpful Links:

Official POTA Website: https://parksontheair.com
POTA Spots: https://pota.app

#parksontheair #pota #hamradio

Think you can get by with just one license for newer Stellantis vehicles? The reality is, if you want to bypass the Secure Gateway or actually clear codes, you need both wiTECH and TechAuthority.

Here is a quick breakdown of why trying to use one without the other leaves your technicians locked out in the bay.

snippet.title: QRP Parks on the Air QRS CW POTA CONTACT W2UD w/ N2OKA ELECRAFT KH1 #cw #hamradio #morsecode
snippet.description:
Parks on the Air (POTA) CW Contact QRS 16wpm down to 12wpm W2UD (in New Jersey) with N2OKA (in New York) from US-6462 Garret Mountain Reservation State Park on 3-15-2026. Using Elecraft KH1 QRP transceiver around 3 or 4 watts into W2LI Magloop on 40 meters. Use some farnsworth to space out characters until you can lower radio keyer speed to help out the other operator.

snippet.title: Ground Spike Magic? | Beating Park Noise with Radials & Choke | POTA CW @ US-11222
snippet.description:
Links to specific QSOs below ⬇️

I headed out for a Parks on the Air activation at US-11222 knowing this park has a reputation for high noise levels (QRN/QRM). My usual setup here is the hamstick on the Subaru POTAMobile, but this time I tried something different.

Instead of operating from the vehicle, I installed a ground spike antenna system in a small grassy area about 50 feet away from the parking lot and ran 100 feet of radials on the ground. I also experimented with adding a different 1:1 chokes at the feedpoint to see if it would help reduce noise coming from nearby electric lines.

The noise was still about S3, but signals were much easier to copy and the hunters came in strong.

After a couple of friendly park visitors during setup, the CW activation was underway and turned into a solid run.

This ground-mounted setup may become my new standard for this park, especially with warmer weather coming.

Every POTA Day is a Great Day!

📻 Activation Results
• 73 QSOs
• 78 Minutes On The Air
• Improved Copy Despite QRN/QRM
• 20m CW Activation
• Thanks to all the Hunters!

🧰 Gear Used
Rig: Yaesu FT-891
Tuner: Yaesu FC-50
Battery: WattCycle 12Ah LiFePO4
Key: Modern Morse Nameless v0.h Paddle
Logger: Ham2k Portable Logger (PoLo)

📡 Antenna Setup
• 17’+ Alpha Antenna Whip
• Ground spike mount
• Multiple radials totaling ~100’
• SWR ~1.5:1 (tuned to 1:1 with FC-50)

snippet.title: How I Got My Technician License and Started Talking on the Air | Wyatt KR4EEC
snippet.description:
Meet Wyatt KR4EEC, a newly licensed ham radio operator who recently passed the Technician license exam and is already diving into the hobby.

Since getting licensed just a couple of weeks ago, he’s been exploring the world of amateur radio — listening to repeaters, checking into nets, talking with other operators, and learning more about how ham radio can support emergency communications and community resilience.

One of the things he appreciated most about Ham Radio Prep was how approachable and easy-to-follow the curriculum was.

According to Wyatt KR4EEC, the course stood out because of:

🎥 Clear and easy-to-understand video lessons
📝 Multiple practice exams to reinforce learning
📊 Immediate feedback on missed questions
📚 A curriculum designed to make technical concepts approachable

The ability to take practice exams and receive real-time feedback helped him understand what areas needed improvement and build confidence before exam day.

Now that he’s licensed and active on the air, he’s excited to continue learning and is already considering studying for the General license — and possibly even the Extra license — using Ham Radio Prep courses.

For anyone thinking about getting started in amateur radio, he believes Ham Radio Prep provides an approachable and effective path into the hobby.

🎓 Whether you’re new to ham radio or upgrading your license, we’ve helped over 60,000 students do the same.

👉 Start learning now: https://www.hamradioprep.com

Try a free lesson, pass the test, and get on the air with confidence!

snippet.title: On the Air Activities - Something for Everyone
snippet.description:
In this video I go over a few "on the air" activities as well as some other amateur radio websites for participating in challenges/awards.

I am glad these websites exist, but don't feel constrained or limited if you don't see something that would be fun to participate in. I think it is best to set your own personal goals and challenges to work towards if you are motivated to do so. 

Get on the air and have fun, in whatever way is best for you :)

snippet.description:
Welcome to the War Watch Intel your home for exploring the captivating world of Open Source Intelligence (OSINT) and real-time monitoring!

Join our community as we track and analyze communications and signals from around the globe, including:

HFGCS Emergency Action Message (EAM) Alerts: Stay updated on military and global communications, including critical alerts and updates from the Emergency Action Message System.

Unknown Radio Signals: Tune into the airwaves to investigate strange and unidentified transmissions from around the world.

Flight Tracking & News: Follow real-time aircraft movements and get in-depth research and analysis on global events.

Police Scanners & HF Radio: Explore a variety of scanners for monitoring local and global situations and high-frequency radio transmissions.

Subscribe and stay informed with the latest intelligence and insights.

Disclaimer: This video is for entertainment and educational purposes only. The content presented is based on publicly available information and personal observations. Do not take anything in this video as factual or official information. The creators are not responsible for any actions taken based on the information provided. Viewer discretion is advised. Always adhere to local laws and regulations regarding the interception and dissemination of radio communications.

#OSINT #HFGCS #EAMS #Alerts #HFRadio #MilitaryMonitoring #LiveStream #RealTimeUpdates #FlightTracking #news #InDepthResearch
